options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true.
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false.
Both statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false.
Both statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true.
solution:
Rutherford's gold foil experiment only proved that electrons are held towards nucleus by electrostatic forces of attraction and move in circular orbits with very high speeds.

Bohr's model gave exact formula for simultaneous calculation of speed & distance of electron from the nucleus, something which was deemed impossible according to Heisenberg.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Number of electrons in a shell of quantum number n=2n2.
Since, 2 electrons are stored in 1 orbital,
Number of orbitals in a shell =n2=52=25.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The energy of a spectral line in the hydrogen spectrum is given by $E = 13.6 \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$ eV.
For the Lyman series, the lowest energy line $L_1$ corresponds to the transition from $n=2$ to $n=1$.
$E(L_1) = 13.6 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right) = 13.6 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{4} \right) = 13.6 \times \frac{3}{4}$ eV.
For the Balmer series, the lowest energy line $B_1$ corresponds to the transition from $n=3$ to $n=2$.
$E(B_1) = 13.6 \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2} \right) = 13.6 \left( \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} \right) = 13.6 \times \frac{5}{36}$ eV.
Given $E(L_1) = x \cdot E(B_1)$, we have:
$13.6 \times \frac{3}{4} = x \times 13.6 \times \frac{5}{36}$
$\frac{3}{4} = x \times \frac{5}{36}$
$x = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{36}{5} = 3 \times \frac{9}{5} = \frac{27}{5} = 5.4$
The value of $x$ is $5.4 = 54 \times 10^{-1}$.
Comparing with the required format $x = \text{integer} \times 10^{-1}$, the integer is 54.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Photon A: $\lambda_A = 400$ nm
$E_A = rac{hc}{\lambda_A} = rac{6.626 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{400 imes 10^{-9}} = 4.97 imes 10^{-19}$ J
Photon B: $ u_B = 10^{16}$ s⁻¹
$E_B = h u_B = 6.626 imes 10^{-34} imes 10^{16} = 6.626 imes 10^{-18}$ J
Photon C: $ ar{ u}_C = 10^4$ cm⁻¹ = $10^6$ m⁻¹
$\lambda_C = 10^{-6}$ m
$E_C = rac{hc}{\lambda_C} = rac{6.626 imes 10^{-34} imes 3 imes 10^8}{10^{-6}} = 1.988 imes 10^{-19}$ J
Order: $E_B > E_A > E_C$

subject: Chemistry
solution: Potassium will have the least energy in its 2s orbital. This is due to the fact that the 2s orbital is closest to the nucleus in potassium because of its greater nuclear charge.
